sentences.text: Richard Grimm.
Muselmann was our term for someone who is about to be the next one to go.
He had cheekbones, no flesh.
He had eyes, but couldn't see.
He had feet.
He couldn't walk.
And hands that were hanging down just limply.
That was a muselmann.
I don't know how this term was born.
But suddenly, it was born and it was there.
The closest that I can relate to is probably nomads in the Sahara, the desert, hungry and thirsty and depleted of strength.
And those were Muslims.
And that's why Muslim German, the muselmann, Muslim.
And probably that's the best that I can guess.
But it's only a guess.
I don't know really.
In any event, he I said to him, Richard, I can do it.
If you just let me, I can do it.
I just need to be washed.
I need a bath.
I need a little cream on my hands.
My hands were bleeding.
My hands were calloused.
And I need to be just restored to what I was in Gutenbrunn.
Well, I'll see what I can do.
There were other dentists in [INAUDIBLE], much more qualified than I was.
I was just so fortunate.
I was just so fortunate.
The following morning, when I was staying in line to be [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] taken to the coal mine, I heard him calling my name.
The dentist, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] forwards.
I ran over with all the strength that I had, I stopped six feet in front of the lager-- at the lagerfuhrer, who was Otto Moll, a man whose cruelty can never be adequately described.
And Richard said to him, this was our dentist in Gutenbrunn.
And he sort of endorsed me with a couple of words that I couldn't understand.
And he asked me to come close to him and show him my hands.
And Richard must have told him that I have such bad hands.
And I opened my palms, and I showed it to him.
And he said, my god, he said, how can you work as a dentist with these hands?
Send him to the Krankenbau for two weeks.
Let him do nothing.
But [GERMAN] the hands, care for his hands.
I couldn't believe this.
I thought, this must be godsend into his mouth.
These words are not his.
Because he was known-- he was the first person to drop the canister of Zyklon B into the so-called showers to gas people.
He was the first one.
And each time he dropped it, the story was that he says, [SPEAKING GERMAN], which means, let them eat it.
That's how little he cared.
He had one glass eye and one of his own.
And his body-- I do have a photograph of him.
And I will show it at the end.
His body was just like a picture postcard of a real Nazi.
And he said to me to go and do nothing for two weeks except care for my hands.
I couldn't believe it.
I wasn't about to take this and cash it in.
Because I knew that if some other SS men would see me do nothing, he would not like it.
So I walked around in the camp, picked up trash from the can, do this, did that, helped here, helped there, just to show that I am useful.
Because if you are seen being just a baggage for the camp, you are no longer a baggage.
You were certainly sent to the main camp for extermination.
And so after two weeks, Otto Moll came one day with a complete, most up-to-date equipment, chair, drills, hand pieces.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Whatever I would need ever in a luxury dental station was on his truck.
And the SS men that he-- that drove the truck-- he came in-- by a motorcycle-- he helped me carry the chair in, carry the-- all the equipment.
Then he even had the name of the Polish dentist from whom he has [GERMAN], this equipment.
Just went in.
And he said [GERMAN], and this is ours.
We're taking it.
That was all that was necessary.
They were the rulers.
And there were books.
And there were manuals.
And there were textbooks that I devoured in the next few, days and read.
And I became to function again as a dentist.
And I was able to help my mother-- my father, help my brother.
And soon Otto Moll became my patient.
He came in one day.
And he wanted me to see that he has some kind of a [INAUDIBLE], he thought, in one of his teeth.
And truly, he had.
He then took the pistol out, then put it to the back where I was standing into my chest.
And I said, Herr Lagerfuhrer, you don't have to be afraid.
Put this away.
You just made me nervous.
I was kind of, at this point, kind of casual with him.
I can't do you anything.
What can I do?
I can stick you with a-- with a scalpel?
Cut you?
And you would kill me.
And you would kill a hundred others.
Why would I do that?
Well, he said, it was just a joke.
A kind of a cruel way, a cruel joke.
And I said, put this gun back into the holster.
And he asked me, he said, I understand you have a brother and a father here.
I said, yes, I do.
And he says to me, well, why didn't you tell me?
I said, it's not unusual.
There are many here who have three of four from one family.
I'm not alone who has.
Yeah, but you are different.
So I said, yes, I do have a father and a brother.
Where are they working?
I said, my father works on the [?
Baustelle. ?] He was working on the-- building barracks, more barracks.
And my brother was working still in the mine.
So he says, well, how old is your father?
Told him 50.
Oh, he's too old for the mine.
Let him come to camp.
Let him-- where would you like him to work?
So I thought for a moment.
I said, my father could be an orderly, took care of a barrack.
Clean it, sweep it.
When the inmates leave, make the beds and things like that.
OK.
Which barrack would you want him to send me?
So I knew that there was a Jewish barrack alteste in block number four.
I said, if it is the same to you, I would like he would-- he goes to the barrack four.
OK.
You tell Grimm that I ordered your father to go to barrack four.
How about your brother?
And I said, well, my brother was a dental technician.
And he probably would be very helpful in the first aid room.
OK.
You tell Grimm to put him to the first aid room.
So that was a miracle, a miracle.
And so I had relatively [? common ?] good times, except that the [INAUDIBLE] gave that was assigned to me, that was the [SPEAKING GERMAN].
[INAUDIBLE].
He was a Jew hater.
And when he came in, he came from Stalingrad.
He was wounded in Stalingrad.
Had a shrapnel in his head.
And when he came in, he started to be very cruel to me.
He asked me questions the first time.
And whatever I answered was incorrect, according to him.
I was lying.
I was doing this.
I was doing that.
And soon I found out that his main object is to mistreat me.
He was in charge of me.
Heinze.
Unterscharfuhrer Heinze.
He was the second one.
The first one really was very un-anti-Semitic and-- and a very liberal person.
He didn't care what I did.
But this one went and tested the instruments.
This wasn't right.
That wasn't right.
And then he had a white glove and went over the sash of the door.

And when he found a speck even, he asked me to go on a chair, stand on my heels, and go up and down in knee bends as long as I could till I finally could not, could not, I couldn't-- I just couldn't anymore.
And he would leave and come back the next day and do the same thing again.
He was really torturing me every single day.
And while he tortured me, he was cleaning his fingernails with my instruments.
And he was saying that it is our [GERMAN].
It is our fault for the Second World War.
We wanted the Second World War.
And Churchill is our-- in our pockets.
And Roosevelt is in our pockets.
And the international Jewish [INAUDIBLE] and the international Jewish banking and international Jewish-- everything was Jewish was bad, until finally I had no choice.
I told Otto Britn, who was our lageraltester at that time.
Grimm was demoted at that time.
It wasn't-- they weren't very long, these, these, these lageraltester.
They could only exist for a while till a whim came and the SS men's-- one SS men's mind, and he said, oh, he's no good.
Let's take such and such.
The higher they rose, the faster they fell.
And the deeper they fell.
Goldberg, for example, died in-- in a coal mine.
And I spoke with Otto Britn, who was German, but an inmate.
And he was a nice man.
I have his photograph as well.
And I saw him after the war.
He-- I told him this story, that he comes in and he cleans his fingernails with my instruments.
And he is torturing me.
And he said, oh, we got him.
He cleans his fingernails with the instruments?
Yes, we got him.
He says, one day I'm going to tell the lagerfuhrer, Otto Moll.
I said, you're crazy.
He's gonna kill me.
By that time, Otto Moll and I were already well-known.
He would never kill me.
I didn't think so.
But I-- I was-- I wasn't quite convinced.
Well, anyhow, one day he called me and he called Heinze.
And he said to me, I understand that the unterscharfuhrer comes in your dental station and cleans his fingernails with your instruments, sterilized instruments.
I said, yes, Herr Lagerfuhrer.
And what do you have to say?
He turned to the Heinze.
What do you have to say?
And Heinze just couldn't understand that here is another SS man, equal to him, perhaps in rank not, but in stature, complaining about him mistreating a Jew.
What's going on here?
He said, I don't have to answer you.
Oh, he says, no?
You're going to be transferred within 24 hours.
And he was.
And in came a Volksdeutsche, [INAUDIBLE], that was my superior from that point on, who couldn't care less what I did.
He couldn't care less who wins the war.
He was a coal miner himself before the war.
He didn't know anything about dentistry and nothing about first aid even.
And I did have a, however, an SS doctor who came and visited me once a week.
I had to keep daily records of what I did, who I treated, and so forth.
And then one day, he asked me to make a bridge.
He treated-- at that time, he was treating the guards and the SS men.
I wasn't.
And he wanted to make a bridge for a SS man.
And he asked me whether I have much gold for a bridge left.
And I said, Herr Lagerfuhrer, how-- where would I have gold?

And in surprise, he looked at me and he said, don't you take the gold out of the inmates, which are dead and transferred to Auschwitz to the main camp or those that are killed in the [INAUDIBLE] or die?
I said, no.
Why don't you?
I said, no one ordered me.
I don't know anything about it.
And he gave me, explicitly, the order to remove gold from those that are in the [GERMAN], which means the dead room.
It was a difficult order to fulfill.
I looked at him and I said to myself, if I refuse, there's no one else here.
I don't know what he'll do to me.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But I still don't know.
Only excusing myself that life is precious, and I said, yes.
And I took a little box that one of the foremen made for me and kept separate instruments for that purpose.
I hope you would spare me to describe what I have gone through when I went to the dead room to extract gold from the teeth.
But those that are interested in knowing, I broadly wrote about it in my book, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It is difficult.
It is sometime even difficult to bring it to your memory.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Not to say, how difficult it is to talk about it.
So I hope you spare me that, Louise.
Well, we were in Furstengrube.
And in time, they transferred all the doctors to the Front, because of so many German doctors were killed on the East Front, on the Russian Front.
And they had so many in their field [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] hospitals, that they needed every dentist and every doctor.
And I was ordered to take care of the guards as well as the inmates.
And that was another shot in the arm.

Because when they came to a dentist, perhaps the fear for the dentist, perhaps of-- out of loyalty, or-- or maybe even at that time, the war wasn't going that great for them, perhaps there was a thought of losing the war, they brought me something to eat.
Food.
A little I asked for it each time that the dentist came, but he brought me a few carpules and a few ampules of Novocain.
But I sort of economized.
And instead of using 2 cc per extraction, as usually it is used in the usual recommendation, I used half of it, depending on which-- what kind of a tooth it was.
And sometime even divide it into three, the same carpule.
They kept-- they, they, they picked it up every Thursday when they came.
[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] The dentist.
Inmates Captors and inmates.
My captors.
And in time, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my father was beaten by the very kapo who I thought would be the best barrack alteste for him, because he was sick.
He couldn't work.
But my father wasn't one to complain.
His name was Nathan Green.
And one day, I was called to the [GERMAN] to the wash room, the wash room.
My father lay there with cold water over his face.
One of the [GERMAN], one of the orderlies from the same block, helped me to bring my father to first aid room.
He didn't survive long.
He never regained consciousness.
Within 48 hours, he died.
He was taken to Auschwitz for cremating.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] He was 51 at the time.
And so we were, my brother and I, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he in the first aid room and I in the dental station.
There were hangings.
There were attempts to escape and be caught.
There were other atrocities, where people dared to try to escape and were shot on the-- as they jumped over the fence.
Life was so cheap.
One could know how much he risked.
But he also knew that he's not losing much by risking.
And many risked.
I remember a good friend of mine, Russian, by the name of Boris, who was a inmate, because he tried to escape already from his [?
D-- ?] from his [GERMAN] from his war camp or prisoner of war camp.
And he came in.
And he said to me-- this was a Saturday.
He said, this afternoon when everybody is asleep-- there was a period where we slept between 2:00 and 4:00-- I am going to go over the fence.
I said, Boris, you're crazy.
You'll never make it.
You'll never make it.
The towers will see you.
Some guard is gonna spot you.
You'll never make it.
I will make it.
I will make it.
He was very hopeful to make it.
He went to the other side.
And he was shot, was killed.
There were a number of lager blockalteste [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that were planning to escape by digging under one block to the outside.
The story went that they were betrayed by one of those that were trying to escape.
I never was able to confirm this or deny this.
But the man who they-- was accused of that by those that supposedly knew was Goldstein.
It was the same man who was in charge of the first aid room in Furstengrube, the first camp.
He was the barber in-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Furstengrube-- who was in Steineck.
He was the barber in Furstengrube, I should say.
Excuse me.
I get confused sometime between those names.
And as I said, there's never been any confirmation.
No one is [? know ?] for sure whether this happened or not.
Was I able to hide the gold?
I was, but I had no purpose to.
There was no reason for it.
What would I do, Louise, with the gold?
What can I do with it?
It was not of any value to me.
It wouldn't have been of any value.
No, I couldn't trade with anyone.
I couldn't go outside from Furstengrube.
Furstengrube was pretty much like Auschwitz.
It was very, very controlled by--
No point in me doing this.
There's no reason for me to do that.
But I did have some gold when we were-- when we left Auschwitz, because in the last two weeks, no one came to pick it up.
So whatever I had, I kept in my pocket when I was let go.
I said, maybe it will help me somewhere where I go.
And so in January, in the middle of January, sometime around 15th or so of January, when the Russians were coming close to Auschwitz, we were all taken out to Auschwitz on a death march.
Out of Furstengrube.
Out of Furstengrube-- the-- out of Auschwitz.
Out of Furstengrube, yeah, out of-- Buna-- Auschwitz III, Furstengrube.
We were marched.
It was a winter.
It was a bad winter.
We had little clothing to shelter us from that kind of frost and with that kind of snow.
And people were just falling in line like the flies.
And whoever could not keep up with the pace set by the SS was just shot on the road, left behind unceremoniously.
They didn't even put them in the-- in the ditch, off the embankment.
They just left them on the road.
And sometime at night when we walked, we came up on people, had to walk around.
Hundreds and hundreds were shot.
I was never able to confirm this, but the story was that we left 64,000 from Buna, from Furstengrube, from [?
Janina, ?] from several of those satellite camps of Buna for Auschwitz III.
And when we arrived in-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Gutenbrunn-- when we arrived in-- in Buchenwald, we were 46,000 or 48,000. 16,000 lost their life on this death march.
[SIGHS] About five days of walking and about eight days.
We were brought to Gleiwitz this time.
Gleiwitz was Germany.
It was a town.
And there we were loaded on-- on open.
This time.
When we left Furstengrube-- when we left, rather, Gutenbrunn in the summer, we were put on closed cars.
This time we were put on open cars.
This was January 1945.
And we were driven, again, on one of these horrible, horrendous trips to Buchenwald.
And again, people were dying in [? groves. ?] When we came to Buchenwald, there wasn't room for us in Buchenwald, because a lot of the other camps were also being liquidated and brought west.
And we were lying just in the field for two or three days.
We didn't have an organized meal time, where we would get bread and soup or coffee.
From time to time, we get something from time to time.
From a day or so, we didn't get anything at all.
Till they put us on the same wagons after three, four days and brought us to Harz Mountain to a camp called Dora Nordhausen.
Dora Nordhausen was a different camp altogether.
Dora Nordhausen was very unusual, not like anything that we have ever seen, because the camp was in the mountain, in the caves of the mountain.
